title: Who Is in the Planning Team?
description: Supply chain planning doesn't happen in isolation. This topic introduces the cast of people who make planning work.
text: Planning is a team sport A planning system doesn't run itself. Behind every forecast, every supply plan, and every S&OP decision is a group of people deciding what the numbers mean and what to do about them. The technology matters, but the people are what turn data into decisions — and they are usually the hardest part of any planning transformation to get right. It's easy to underestimate this. New tools get bought, processes get redesigned, dashboards get built — and then the same people are asked to work in a fundamentally different way overnight. Systems can be configured in weeks. Changing how people think, what they own, and how they trust each other takes far longer. That's why most planning programmes that struggle don't fail on the software; they fail on the people side. Who owns supply chain planning? Ownership rarely sits with one person. Planning is inherently cross functional — it lives at the intersection of commercial, operations, and finance. Someone has to hold the whole thing together, but no single function can run it alone. In practice the team splits into two layers: The core group does the day to day planning work: Demand planners own the forecast — the statistical baseline plus the market intelligence layered on top. Supply planners turn that demand signal into a feasible supply response, working within capacity, inventory, and lead time constraints. The S&OP lead owns the end to end cycle, facilitates the cross functional reviews, and drives the team to a single agreed plan. The extended team provides essential input but doesn't live in the plan day to day: Finance validates that the volume plan ties back to the financial plan and the P&L. Commercial brings the voice of the market — customer commitments, promotions, and new product launches. IT keeps the platform, integrations, and data pipelines running. title: The Planning Org Chart
description: See how the planning team is structured, who reports to whom, and where cross-functional roles connect.
text: What this chart shows The diagram below maps a typical supply chain planning organisation. The S&OP Lead sits at the top, not because they manage everyone, but because they own the process that ties the team together. Reporting into the planning function are the demand and supply lines — each with a manager and the planners who do the day to day work — alongside a planning analyst who supports both sides with data and reporting. Off to the side sit the cross functional roles : Finance and Commercial. These people don't report into the planning team, but the plan can't be agreed without them. They join the S&OP reviews, bring their function's perspective, and own specific inputs to the plan. How to use it Click any role to see its core responsibilities and the competencies that role needs. Use it to understand where accountability sits, where the handoffs between roles happen, and where your own role fits in the wider picture. Every organisation structures this slightly differently — treat it as a reference model rather than a rigid template.

title: Building an Effective Planning Team
description: What separates high-performing planning teams from the rest — and the practical steps to get there.
text: Right size the team to the business There is no universal headcount for a planning team. A single site business with a few hundred SKUs might run with one demand planner, one supply planner, and a part time S&OP lead. A global business with thousands of SKUs across dozens of plants needs dedicated managers, multiple planners per region, and analysts to keep the data clean. The mistake is copying another company's structure without matching it to your own complexity — either drowning a small business in roles it doesn't need, or stretching three people across a problem that needs ten. Clear ownership beats shared ownership High performing teams have sharp lines of accountability. Each role owns a specific output — the forecast, the supply plan, the financial reconciliation — and everyone knows who that is. Struggling teams tend to have the opposite: everyone touches everything, nobody owns anything, and when a number is wrong there's no single person responsible for fixing it. "The team owns the plan" sounds collaborative, but in practice it means no one does. Common failure modes A few patterns show up again and again in teams that underperform: Planners spending 80% of their time gathering and cleaning data instead of analysing it and making decisions. If your skilled planners are effectively manual data integrators, the value of the role is wasted. No dedicated S&OP lead. When the cycle is run "on the side" by someone with another full time job, the cross functional alignment quietly degrades and reviews become status updates instead of decisions. Demand and supply working in silos. When the two sides only meet in the formal review, problems surface too late to act on. No analyst layer , leaving managers and planners to maintain master data and reporting themselves. Practical steps to build a strong team 1. Map roles to outcomes, not job titles. title: The Broader Organisation Structure
description: The planning team sits within a larger organisational context. Understanding how the company is structured — and where planning fits — clarifies decision-making authority and stakeholder relationships.
text: Why organisational structure matters for planners Planning does not happen in isolation. Every planning decision touches other functions: a supply constraint affects what sales can commit to; a demand forecast depends on inputs from commercial; an allocation decision requires sign off from leadership. Knowing who sits where in the organisation — and what authority they hold — determines how planning decisions get made and escalated. A planner who understands the organisational structure makes better decisions faster: they know who to call when a supply issue needs commercial input, who needs to approve an exception, and whose sign off is required before a scenario is promoted to the baseline. The functional structure relevant to planning Planning typically sits within the Supply Chain or Operations function, but interacts heavily with four others: Commercial / Sales — owns the demand signal. The commercial team manages customer relationships, holds the sales forecast, and makes commitments to customers. In S&OP, the commercial team presents the demand review. Alignment between planning and commercial on the demand signal is foundational to a credible plan. Operations / Manufacturing — owns supply execution. The operations team runs the production processes, manages the production schedule, and reports on actual output. Planning creates the proposed production schedule; operations executes it and feeds back deviations. Finance — owns the financial plan. The financial plan (budget, reforecast) needs to be aligned with the operational plan produced in S&OP. Finance participates in the executive S&OP to ensure that the agreed plan is financially sound and consistent with approved budgets. Procurement — owns supplier relationships. Procurement negotiates contracts, manages supplier performance, and resolves supply issues. Planning signals what needs to be ordered; procurement determines whether the supply base can deliver it. title: Quarter in the Life of a Strategic Planner
description: A structured walkthrough of how a strategic planner spends a typical quarter — anchored by three monthly S&OP cycles plus a quarterly IBP or business review horizon.
text: Overview The strategic planner operates on the longest planning horizon — typically 12–18 months out, with the monthly S&OP cycle as the primary drumbeat. A quarter contains three full S&OP cycles. Each month, the strategic planner runs or coordinates the full S&OP process: demand review, supply review, inventory review, resource review, and executive S&OP. The quarter level lens adds a layer above that: reviewing whether the rolling plan is converging on the business target, identifying structural risks that span beyond the monthly horizon, and feeding into any quarterly business review or IBP refresh. title: Week in the Life of an Operational Planner
description: A structured walkthrough of how an operational planner spends a typical week — from Monday's order prioritisation through to Friday's execution feedback loop.
text: Overview The operational planner lives in the short term horizon — typically the next 1–4 weeks. Their week is shaped by a recurring rhythm of monitoring, exception resolution, and actuals capture. No two days are identical, but the underlying structure is predictable: check what has changed overnight, resolve what needs resolving, and close out what was promised. title: Month in the Life of a Tactical Planner
description: A structured walkthrough of how a tactical planner spends a typical month — from S&OE cadence management through to handover into the monthly S&OP cycle.
text: Overview The tactical planner bridges the gap between strategic intent and operational reality. Their month is anchored by two rhythms running in parallel: the weekly S&OE cycle (monitoring, exceptions, integrated review) and the monthly handover into S&OP (feeding actuals, flagging risks, aligning the near term plan with the longer term picture). Their job is to keep the near term plan credible and executable while the monthly plan is being shaped above them. title: What Is RASCI?
description: RASCI is a simple framework for assigning accountability in planning processes. This topic explains what it means and why it matters.
text: The Five Letters RASCI is a way to make accountability explicit. Every step in a process gets mapped against the people involved, and each person is assigned one of five roles: Responsible — the person who does the work to complete the step. Accountable — the single person who owns the outcome and signs it off. Supporting — people who actively help the Responsible party get the work done. Consulted — people whose input is sought before the step is finished; this is two way dialogue. Informed — people who are told the outcome after the fact; this is one way communication. R Versus A The distinction that trips teams up most is the difference between Responsible and Accountable. Responsible is the doer — the hands on the keyboard, the person building the forecast or running the capacity check. Accountable is the decision authority — the person who answers for whether the step was done well and on time. There can be several people Responsible for a step, but there should only ever be one person Accountable. If two people are Accountable, nobody is, because each can point at the other when something slips. Why Planning Teams Need This Planning is a chain of handoffs across functions: demand passes a forecast to supply, supply passes a plan to finance, finance reconciles it for the executive review. Each handoff is a place where ownership can quietly evaporate. When accountability is implicit, steps get skipped, decisions stall waiting for someone to make a call, and the same arguments resurface every cycle. A RASCI matrix removes the ambiguity. It states, in advance, exactly who does the work, who decides, who is asked, and who is simply kept in the loop. How to Read the Matrix A RASCI matrix is a grid. Process steps run down the left as rows, and the roles or people run across the top as columns. Each cell holds a letter — or is left blank if that person has no part in that step. To understand any step, read across its row: the single A tells you who owns it, the R (or

title: Making RASCI Work in Practice
description: A RASCI is only as good as the behaviour it drives. This topic covers how to implement and sustain it.
text: A Matrix Is Not the Goal Plenty of organisations build a RASCI, paste it into a slide, and never look at it again. The document is not the point — the behaviour it drives is. A matrix that nobody references during the actual planning cycle is just decoration. Treat it as a working tool, not an artefact. Common Failure Modes Three patterns sink most RASCI efforts: Everyone is Responsible for everything. When a column is a wall of Rs, accountability has been spread so thin it no longer exists. If everyone owns the step, nobody truly does, and work falls through the gaps. No single Accountable. Either a step has no A at all, leaving it ownerless, or it has two, which guarantees finger pointing when something goes wrong. Every step needs exactly one A — no more, no less. Defined but ignored in meetings. The matrix says the Demand Planner is Consulted, yet the review proceeds without their input. Once the RASCI and the real behaviour diverge, the matrix loses all authority and the team stops trusting it. Four Steps to Make It Stick 1. Review it with the team regularly. Walk through the matrix at the start of an implementation and revisit it every few cycles. Shared understanding only forms when people see it together and agree out loud who owns what. 2. Use it to resolve meeting conflicts. When two people both think a decision is theirs, pull up the RASCI. The person marked A makes the call. This turns the matrix from a poster into a live arbitration tool. 3. Start simple and evolve. Do not try to map every micro task on day one. Begin with the major steps of each S&OP stage, get those right, and add detail only where ambiguity is actually causing friction. 4. Hold people to their letter. If someone is Accountable, make them present the outcome. If someone is Consulted, make sure they are genuinely asked. Accountability only becomes real when it shows up in how meetings are run and how decisions are made. Done well, a RASCI shortens meetings, ends recurring ownership arg

title: The Authority Model
description: Who can make which planning decisions, at what threshold, and what delegation looks like in practice.
text: Two Kinds of Authority Not all planning decisions are the same. The first kind is authority to act within a plan — making routine calls inside the boundaries that the plan has already set. A supply planner releasing a purchase order within an agreed quantity range is acting within authority. A demand planner adjusting a short term forecast inside a tolerance band is doing the same. These decisions belong to the person doing the work, and they do not require sign off. The second kind is authority to change the plan itself — adjusting targets, overriding constraints, or committing to actions that fall outside what was agreed in the last S&OP cycle. This is a different category entirely. These decisions have knock on effects across functions and need to be owned at the right level. Confusing the two is where most authority problems begin. When planners escalate routine acts within authority because nobody ever clarified that they were empowered to make them, meetings slow down and decision making bottlenecks at management. When people change the plan without the authority to do so, commitments made in the executive S&OP quietly unravel at the operational level. Setting Decision Thresholds An authority model works by attaching thresholds to decision types. A threshold defines the point at which a decision must be escalated rather than resolved locally. Common threshold dimensions in planning include: Volume or value — orders above a quantity or cost limit need approval from the next level up Time horizon — changes to the frozen zone (typically the nearest 4–6 weeks of plan) require escalation; changes beyond it do not Deviation from plan — a forecast revision that exceeds a defined percentage of the baseline needs sign off; smaller revisions do not Cross functional impact — any action that requires another function to change their own plan must be agreed jointly, not decided unilaterally These thresholds do not need to be elaborate. title: Sustaining Ownership Over Time
description: How planning accountability is enforced and kept alive beyond the initial RASCI definition.
text: The Definition Problem Most planning teams can point to a RASCI. Far fewer have a RASCI that still reflects how decisions actually get made six months after implementation. The gap is not unusual — it is the default outcome when accountability is treated as something you define once and then leave alone. Ownership drifts for practical reasons. People change roles. Processes get adjusted without updating the matrix. A shortcut taken during a busy period becomes the new normal. A meeting gets restructured and the Accountable person stops attending. None of these changes are announced; the matrix just quietly stops matching reality. Keeping the Matrix Alive A RASCI needs a review cadence to stay current. This does not require a dedicated governance meeting — a fifteen minute agenda item at the quarterly S&OP review is usually sufficient. The questions to answer are simple: does the matrix still reflect who is actually making each decision? Have roles changed? Have any new decision types emerged that are not covered? The review is also the right moment to catch silent drift — cases where the person marked Accountable has effectively handed off the decision without the matrix being updated. When drift is identified, update the matrix immediately. The longer it runs out of date, the less authority it carries, and the less likely anyone is to consult it when a conflict arises. Linking Accountability to Meeting Structure The most effective way to keep ownership real is to make it visible in the meeting structure. If the Demand Review RASCI assigns Accountability to the Demand Manager, then the Demand Manager chairs the review and presents the output. If the Supply Review marks the Supply Planner as Responsible, the Supply Planner does not send a deputy unless one has been formally named. This connection between the RASCI and who shows up, who presents, and who makes the final call is what turns a document into a living operating model. When the two diverge — when the person

title: Planning Rhythm Overview
description: The S&OP and S&OE drumbeat — how the three planning horizons connect and how the cadence calendar governs when planning work happens.
text: The Rhythm Behind the Plan Planning is not a single event — it is a system of recurring activities happening at different frequencies and different time horizons simultaneously. The operational team is adjusting today's execution. The tactical team is managing this week and next. The strategic team is shaping the next 12–18 months. All three are running at once, each feeding into the others. What holds this together is cadence: a predictable calendar of when planning activities happen, who is involved, and what decisions get made. Without cadence, each horizon operates in isolation — the monthly plan disconnects from weekly reality, and daily execution drifts from both. Three Horizons, Three Rhythms The planning function operates across three horizons, each with its own drumbeat. Operational — weekly This is the execution horizon: the next 1–4 weeks. Demand and supply signals are reviewed daily or weekly. Exceptions are triaged and resolved. Actuals are captured in ERP. The key process here is S&OE (Sales and Operations Execution), which runs on a weekly cadence and culminates in an integrated review where cross functional exceptions are resolved or escalated. Tactical — monthly This is the near term management horizon: roughly the next 1–3 months. The tactical planner's job is to keep the near term plan credible — disaggregating the monthly plan from S&OP into executable weekly buckets, managing constraints as they surface, and feeding actuals back upward into the next S&OP cycle. The rhythm here is shaped by the monthly S&OE close and S&OP handover. Strategic — quarterly and beyond This is the S&OP horizon: typically 12–18 months out, reviewed monthly. Each month, the strategic planner runs the full S&OP cycle — demand review, supply review, pre S&OP, and executive S&OP — and closes a new monthly plan. title: What Is S&OP?
description: Sales & Operations Planning is the monthly process that aligns demand, supply, and finance into a single agreed plan — the authoritative version of what the business intends to do.
text: The core purpose of S&OP Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP) is a structured monthly process that brings together demand, supply, inventory, and financial plans into one agreed upon view of the business. Its purpose is alignment: ensuring that sales commitments are backed by supply capacity, that financial targets are grounded in operational reality, and that cross functional decisions are made together rather than in silos. S&OP operates at an aggregate level — typically by product family, region, and month. It is not a day to day execution process. It sets the constraints and priorities within which weekly S&OE and daily execution happen. What S&OP is not S&OP is not a forecasting meeting. The forecast is an input, not the output. S&OP is also not an operations review — it does not manage last week's shipments or resolve today's quality issue. Those belong in S&OE. Confusing the two is a common failure mode: executive meetings get dragged into day to day firefighting, and the long term plan never gets the structured attention it needs. Going deeper The S&OP Process module covers the full monthly cycle in depth — the demand review, supply review, pre S&OP reconciliation, executive sign off, roles, outputs, and common failure modes.

title: What Is S&OE?
description: Sales & Operations Execution is the weekly process that manages the near-term horizon — resolving exceptions, adjusting short-term supply, and keeping execution aligned with the S&OP plan.
text: S&OE: the bridge between planning and execution Sales & Operations Execution (S&OE) is the weekly cadence that sits between the monthly S&OP plan and day to day operational execution. Where S&OP sets the plan for the next 3–18 months, S&OE manages the next 1–6 weeks — the horizon where the plan meets reality. S&OE is the process where planners answer: "Given what we agreed last month, what's going wrong this week, and what do we do about it?" Its output is not a new plan — it is corrective action within the bounds of the existing S&OP plan. How S&OP and S&OE relate The two processes are complementary, not competing. They operate on different time horizons at different levels of detail: | | S&OP | S&OE | | | | | | Horizon | 3–18 months | 1–6 weeks | | Granularity | Product family / month | SKU / week | | Cadence | Monthly | Weekly | | Purpose | Align demand, supply, finance | Resolve near term exceptions | | Output | Agreed plan | Corrective actions | S&OP without S&OE creates plans that drift from reality within days. S&OE without S&OP creates reactive firefighting without direction. Going deeper The S&OE Process module covers the full weekly cycle in depth — the exception queue, weekly review structure, adjustment decisions, roles, and how to escalate back to S&OP when a deviation is too large to resolve tactically.

title: Execution: Translating Plans into Action
description: Execution is where the plan becomes reality — purchase orders, production runs, shipments. Understanding how plans flow into execution systems prevents the gap between what was planned and what was done.
text: The execution layer Execution is the operational layer where plans become physical actions: purchase orders are placed with suppliers, production batches are started, shipments are dispatched. In the operating model, execution operates at the shortest time horizon — days and shifts rather than weeks and months. Execution does not decide what to do. That was determined in S&OP and refined in S&OE. Execution's job is to deliver the confirmed plan accurately and feed outcomes back so the planning model stays current. The planner's role in execution Planners do not execute — they enable execution. The planner's responsibility is to ensure that the plan in Planning software is current and credible, that planned orders are released to ERP on time, and that execution deviations are surfaced and reconciled promptly in S&OE. Where execution is structurally unable to follow the plan, the plan is revised and the deviation is escalated. Going deeper The Execution Process module covers the daily execution layer in full — order prioritisation, real time visibility, execution discipline, and the feedback loop that keeps planning actuals current.

title: The Planning Cadence
description: A well-designed meeting rhythm connects monthly S&OP, weekly S&OE, and daily execution. The cadence is the operating model in practice — the recurring structure that keeps the organisation aligned.
text: What a cadence is A planning cadence is the recurring schedule of meetings, reviews, and decisions that keep the planning cycle running. It is not just a calendar — it is the operational backbone of the planning operating model. A strong cadence ensures that the right decisions are made at the right frequency by the right people. Without a cadence, planning degrades into ad hoc firefighting: whoever shouts loudest gets attention, long term issues never get reviewed, and plans drift from reality without anyone noticing. The three level cadence A standard planning cadence has three levels that nest together: Monthly — S&OP cycle Demand review (Week 1 of the month) Supply review (Week 2) Pre S&OP reconciliation (Week 3) Executive S&OP sign off (Week 4) Plan publication and ERP update (end of month) Weekly — S&OE cycle Exception review: items at risk in the 1–6 week horizon Corrective action assignment and tracking Short horizon capacity and supply resolution Update to Planning software planning model where needed Daily — Execution layer Production order status review Inbound shipment tracking Customer order fulfilment monitoring Exception escalation to S&OE where the issue cannot be resolved within the day Designing the cadence for your organisation The cadence above is a template, not a prescription. In practice, the right cadence depends on the length of your planning horizon, the variability of your demand and supply, and the size and structure of your planning team. For agricultural businesses with long growing cycles, the monthly S&OP cycle often needs to be supplemented with seasonal planning reviews — deep dives that happen quarterly or at key crop cycle milestones rather than on a calendar month basis. title: The Three Planning Horizons
description: Planning operates across three distinct time horizons — long-term strategic, mid-term tactical, and short-term operational — each with different owners, decisions, and degrees of certainty.
text: Why horizons matter Not all planning decisions are the same. Some decisions — like which markets to serve or how much capacity to build — take months to implement and years to pay off. Others — like which orders to pick today — need to be made and acted on within hours. Trying to manage all of these with a single process or cadence creates noise at best and paralysis at worst. Planning horizons exist to match the right level of decision making to the right time frame. Each horizon has different data, different certainty, and different owners. Understanding which horizon you are working in is one of the most practical skills a planner can develop. Long term: strategic horizon (12–36 months) The long term horizon covers roughly 12 to 36 months out, though this varies by industry. At this horizon, the focus is on structural decisions: capacity investments, network design, new product introductions, market expansion, and supplier contracts. Decisions here are low frequency but high impact. The forecast is uncertain — you are working with assumptions and scenarios, not a precise number. That uncertainty is acceptable because the decisions being made are about direction, not precision. Ownership at this horizon sits with senior leadership and, in some organisations, a dedicated strategic planning function. The finance plan and the sales plan converge here into a long range business plan. Mid term: tactical horizon (3–12 months) The tactical horizon is where S&OP lives. Over a 3 to 12 month window, the plan becomes specific enough to drive real commitments: production schedules, replenishment orders, workforce adjustments, and budget allocation. This is still an aggregate level plan — by product family and month, not by SKU and day — but it is specific enough to test against actual capacity and supply constraints. The S&OP cycle updates this plan monthly, balancing demand and supply and surfacing gaps before they become crises. Ownership at this horizon is cross functional

title: Time Fences
description: Time fences define how much the plan can change within each zone of the planning horizon — protecting operational stability while keeping flexibility where it still has value.
text: What a time fence is A time fence is a boundary within the planning horizon that defines the rules for making changes to the plan. Inside a fence, changes are restricted or prohibited. Outside it, changes are expected and accommodated. The logic is straightforward: the closer an event is, the more expensive it becomes to change. Shifting a production run scheduled for tomorrow has real cost — materials may already be staged, labour is rostered, and customers may be expecting delivery. Changing a plan for six months from now costs almost nothing. Time fences make this economic reality explicit. The three zones Most planning systems recognise three zones, each with different change rules. The firm fence is the innermost zone — typically zero to four weeks, though this varies by industry and lead time. Inside the firm fence, the plan is frozen. Changes require explicit authorisation from a senior planner or operations manager, and they come with an understood cost. This zone protects the execution team from constant replanning and gives supply chain operations the stability to function efficiently. The trade off fence is the middle zone — roughly four to twelve weeks. Changes here are possible but require a conscious trade off. Adding volume requires confirming that capacity exists or that something else will give way. The constraint is real, but it can be negotiated. This is where S&OE operates — monitoring and adjusting within what was agreed in S&OP, escalating where adjustments require cross functional decisions. The open fence covers everything beyond the trade off fence. In this zone, the plan is a working assumption, not a commitment. Changes are expected as forecasts update, commercial conditions shift, or strategic priorities evolve. S&OP reviews live here, taking the plan forward and making the assumptions explicit. title: Horizon to Cycle Mapping
description: Each planning horizon is managed by a specific process cycle — S&OP owns the mid-to-long horizon, S&OE the near-term, and execution the immediate — and decisions flow and sharpen as they move down.
text: The connection between horizons and cycles The three planning horizons — long term, mid term, and short term — are not just conceptual boundaries. Each one is actively managed by a process cycle with its own cadence, participants, and outputs. Getting this mapping right is what turns the horizon model from theory into a functioning operating system. S&OP manages the mid to long horizon Sales & Operations Planning is the cycle that owns the tactical and strategic horizon. Its monthly cadence is suited to decisions that play out over months: aggregate supply and demand balance, inventory strategy, capacity commitments, and financial alignment. S&OP sets the authoritative plan for roughly 3 to 18 months out. It is the cycle where cross functional trade offs are made at an aggregate level — by product family, region, and period. The output is not a detailed schedule; it is a set of agreed constraints and priorities that the next cycle down can work within. When S&OP is running well, the mid term horizon is covered. Gaps between demand and supply are visible months before they become crises. Financial and operational plans are reconciled. Escalations happen early enough that options still exist. S&OE manages the near term horizon Sales & Operations Execution is the cycle that owns the operational horizon — roughly zero to twelve weeks. Where S&OP sets the plan, S&OE monitors whether the plan is holding and makes the adjustments needed to stay on track. S&OE runs weekly. Its participants are operational planners rather than senior leadership, and its focus is exception management: what has changed since the last cycle, what is at risk, and what needs to be escalated to S&OP versus resolved within the existing plan. S&OE works within the commitments S&OP has made. It does not reconfigure the supply network or rebalance the financial plan — it executes and adapts within the boundary set one level up. Execution manages the immediate Daily execution — order fulfilment, produc

title: What Is Integrated Planning?
description: Integrated planning means demand, supply, inventory, finance, and operations are planned together in a single connected system — not in separate silos that are reconciled after the fact.
text: The core principle Integrated planning is the discipline of planning demand, supply, inventory, finance, and operations as a single connected system rather than as separate functions that periodically share spreadsheets. The principle sounds obvious. In practice, most organisations do not do it. Demand planning runs its forecast. Supply planning runs its own numbers. Finance builds a budget from different assumptions. Operations makes its own capacity decisions. Each function is optimising locally — and the organisation as a whole pays the price through excess inventory, missed service targets, and financial surprises. Integration does not mean everyone agrees all the time. It means decisions made in one part of the plan are immediately visible to, and constrained by, decisions in every other part. Why siloed planning fails When planning functions work in isolation, a predictable set of failures emerges. Demand and supply plans diverge. The commercial team commits to volume that supply cannot support. Supply builds inventory for demand that will not materialise. Neither team knows about the gap until the shortfall or the write off appears. Financial and operational plans contradict each other. The budget assumes a cost structure that the supply plan cannot achieve. The S&OP process produces a volume plan that finance has not modelled. Month end closes become exercises in explaining variances rather than managing the business. Inventory becomes a buffer for dysfunction rather than a strategic asset. Safety stock is set to cover the uncertainty created by poor handoffs between functions, not genuine market volatility. What integration actually requires Integration is not a technology project, though good planning software makes it much easier. It requires three things. A shared data model. Demand, supply, inventory, and financial plans must live in a connected data structure where a change in one propagates to the others. If the demand planner updates the forecast in

title: The Planning Hierarchy
description: The planning hierarchy — strategic plan, S&OP, S&OE, execution — defines how decisions flow down from broad direction to daily action, with each level setting constraints for the one below.
text: A hierarchy of decisions Integrated planning is not a flat structure. It is organised as a hierarchy of four levels, each making decisions at a different level of resolution, over a different time horizon, and with a different cadence. Understanding this hierarchy is essential to understanding why the system works when it works — and why it breaks down when levels are confused or bypassed. The four levels, from top to bottom, are: strategic plan, Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP), Sales & Operations Execution (S&OE), and execution. Strategic plan At the top of the hierarchy sits the strategic plan. This is typically an annual or multi year exercise that sets the direction for the business: which markets to compete in, what capacity investments to make, what service and cost targets to achieve. The strategic plan does not produce a production schedule. It produces boundaries — volume ranges, capital budgets, network configurations — within which the rest of the hierarchy operates. These boundaries change slowly and require senior leadership decisions. S&OP S&OP translates the strategic plan into a monthly rolling plan for the mid term horizon — typically 3 to 18 months. It balances aggregate demand and supply, sets inventory targets, aligns with the financial plan, and produces a single agreed view of what the business will do. S&OP operates at an aggregate level: product families, regions, monthly buckets. Its output is not a detailed schedule but a set of constraints and targets that S&OE can work within. It is the primary mechanism by which cross functional trade offs are made explicitly rather than left to chance. Every decision in S&OP is constrained by the strategic plan above it. Every decision S&OE makes must fit within the plan S&OP has set. S&OE S&OE takes the S&OP plan and manages its execution over the near term horizon — typically zero to twelve weeks. Running weekly, it monitors whether the plan is on track, identifies deviations and risks, and makes t

title: Handoffs and Dependencies
description: Three critical handoffs connect the planning levels — S&OP to S&OE, S&OE to execution, and execution back to planning. Poor handoffs are the most common source of planning dysfunction.
text: Where integration lives or dies A planning hierarchy with well designed levels can still fail if the handoffs between those levels are poor. A handoff is the point where one level's output becomes the next level's input. It requires a clear protocol: what information is transferred, in what format, at what cadence, and who owns any gaps between what was planned and what was received. There are three critical handoffs in the integrated planning model. Handoff 1: S&OP to S&OE At the end of each S&OP cycle, the agreed monthly plan — covering volume by product family, supply targets, inventory positions, and financial commitments — becomes the working plan for S&OE. S&OE receives this plan and translates it into weekly operational targets. It breaks aggregate volume into SKU level weekly buckets, confirms the targets against current inventory and supplier positions, and identifies any gaps between what S&OP assumed and what is actually available. This handoff fails when S&OP produces a plan at too high a level of aggregation to be actionable, when the plan is not made available to S&OE in a timely way, or when S&OE routinely overrides the S&OP plan without escalation. When it breaks down, S&OE becomes an improvising function rather than an executing one — and the S&OP process loses credibility. Handoff 2: S&OE to execution Each week, S&OE produces a firm near term plan: production priorities, replenishment orders, delivery commitments. This plan is handed to execution — the shop floor, the warehouse, the procurement team — as the authoritative set of priorities for the coming days. For execution to work with this plan, it needs to be both specific enough to act on and stable enough to trust. A plan that changes every day provides neither. The time fence structure exists precisely to protect this handoff: the firm fence ensures that what execution receives on Monday has not been revised by Tuesday. This handoff fails when S&OE does not produce a clear priority list, when

title: The S&OP Cycle
description: A standard S&OP cycle runs monthly through four or five structured steps — from statistical forecast to executive sign-off.
text: How the monthly cycle works A standard S&OP cycle runs monthly and progresses through a defined sequence of reviews. Each step builds on the previous: data is prepared, reviewed by one function, handed to the next, and escalated only where decisions cannot be made at a lower level. Step 1 — Statistical refresh The planning team updates the statistical baseline forecast using the latest demand signals: actuals, point of sale data, orders, and market intelligence. This is the starting point for the demand review, not the output of it. Step 2 — Demand review The commercial team — sales, marketing, key account managers — reviews the statistical baseline and overlays their judgment. They adjust for known events: promotions, new product launches, customer wins or losses, seasonal shifts. The output is a consensus demand plan with agreed volumes and a documented list of assumptions. Step 3 — Supply review Operations reviews whether supply capacity can meet the consensus demand. This includes manufacturing, procurement, logistics, and inventory. The supply team identifies gaps: where supply falls short, where there is excess capacity, and at what cost different options can close the gaps. Step 4 — Pre S&OP (integrated reconciliation) Demand and supply leads meet before the executive review to resolve gaps that are within their authority. Trade off options are prepared for any remaining gaps: build ahead, accept a service level miss, adjust the demand plan, invest in capacity. The pre S&OP ends with a clear list of items requiring executive decision. Step 5 — Executive S&OP Leadership reviews unresolved gaps, approves trade offs, and signs off on the plan. This is a decision meeting, not a status update. Decisions made here are binding: they cascade into procurement commitments, capacity investments, and financial forecasts. The role of time horizons S&OP typically covers a rolling 18–24 month horizon, with the greatest detail in the near term (months 1–3) and more aggregate

title: S&OP Roles and Participants
description: S&OP requires committed participation from multiple functions. Understanding who does what — and who owns the process — is essential to making it work.
text: The S&OP process owner Every S&OP process needs an owner — typically the VP of Supply Chain or COO, supported by a dedicated S&OP manager or IBP leader. The process owner is responsible for the calendar, the data quality, the meeting facilitation, and the follow up on decisions. Without a clear owner, S&OP becomes a series of disconnected reviews with no accountability for outcomes. Demand side participants The demand review is led by the commercial organisation: Sales — provides customer level insights, large order visibility, and win/loss intelligence Marketing — owns promotion plans, new product launches, and campaign timing Demand planning — manages the statistical baseline and reconciles commercial adjustments into a single number Finance — validates that the demand plan aligns with revenue targets and flags material gaps The output from demand side participants is a single consensus number per planning bucket — not a range of opinions. Supply side participants The supply review is led by operations: Manufacturing / production planning — assesses capacity against the consensus demand plan Procurement — evaluates supplier lead times, commitments, and material availability Logistics — assesses distribution capacity, warehousing, and transportation constraints Inventory management — tracks current stock levels, projected coverage, and reorder points Executive participants The executive S&OP involves the senior leadership team: CEO, CFO, CSO, COO, and relevant business unit heads. Their role is to make the decisions that operational teams cannot — trade offs between revenue, cost, service level, and capital. The S&OP manager role In organisations running planning software, the S&OP manager plays a critical technical role: maintaining the planning model, ensuring data integrity, preparing scenario analyses for each review, and publishing the agreed plan back into operating systems. This role bridges the process and the technology. Common failure: missing the right p

title: S&OP Outputs and Decisions
description: The value of S&OP is in what it produces — decisions, commitments, and a plan that the whole business can act on.
text: The consensus plan The primary output of S&OP is the consensus plan: a set of agreed volumes, revenue projections, inventory levels, and supply commitments that the business will operate to until the next cycle. The consensus plan is the single version of truth. Every function — sales, operations, finance — should be able to point to the same numbers. Decision log Every S&OP review should produce a decision log: a written record of decisions made, trade offs approved, and issues deferred. The decision log is critical for two reasons: 1. Accountability — decisions without an owner and a date are not decisions; they are conversations 2. Continuity — the log creates an auditable trail that prevents the same issue from being relitigated every month Actions and follow ups Decisions generate actions. The executive S&OP ends with a list of actions, owners, and due dates. At the start of the next cycle, the first agenda item is a review of open actions from the previous month. S&OP loses credibility quickly if decisions are made but never followed through. Financial bridge A mature S&OP process includes a financial bridge — a reconciliation that shows how the operational plan translates into revenue, cost, and margin. This bridges the gap between the supply chain view (units, volume, coverage) and the finance view (£, margin %). Without this bridge, S&OP and the budget remain two separate processes that never talk to each other. Updated planning horizons The agreed plan is published back into planning software and ERP systems. Near term months become the reference for S&OE and execution. Outer months update inventory targets, procurement commitments, and capacity plans. Publishing the plan is not optional — if downstream processes are not updated, the cycle has not produced anything useful.

title: S&OP in Planning Software
description: Planning software provides the data foundation, scenario modelling, and plan publishing capabilities that make S&OP analysis faster and decisions more grounded in reality.
text: What planning software adds to S&OP A spreadsheet based S&OP is slow to prepare, hard to trust, and difficult to update when assumptions change. Planning software replaces the manual assembly of demand, supply, and inventory data with a live planning model — one that updates automatically as inputs change and lets planners run scenarios in minutes rather than days. The demand review in planning software Before the demand review, planners use the system to: Run the statistical baseline forecast using the configured demand sensing model Load commercial overrides from sales and marketing into a structured input layer Compare the adjusted forecast to the prior month and to budget Produce exception reports — items where the forecast has changed significantly and needs explanation During the review, participants view dashboards that show forecast versus actuals, bias trends, and coverage by SKU or product family. Adjustments are made directly in the system, not in a spreadsheet that gets emailed around. The supply review in planning software Planning software runs a constrained supply plan against the consensus demand: it checks available capacity, supplier lead times, and current inventory, then projects where shortfalls and surpluses will occur. The supply team reviews a pre calculated gap report — not a manually assembled one — which makes the review faster and the gaps harder to dispute. Scenario analysis for pre S&OP Pre S&OP requires trade off analysis: what happens if we accept a 5% service level miss versus building 3 weeks of forward inventory? Planning software lets planners create and compare scenarios against a baseline plan, with the financial impact calculated automatically. These scenarios go into the executive review as structured options, not anecdotal estimates. Publishing the agreed plan Once the executive decision is made, the planning software publishes the agreed plan: inventory targets are updated, procurement orders are confirmed, and the S&OE team

title: Data Collection & Cleansing
description: Gather sales actuals from ERP and field actuals from FMS. Remove anomalies caused by one-off events such as promotions or supply failures. Validate that all required data feeds are complete before the statistical model is run.

title: Statistical Baseline Generation
description: Run the statistical forecasting model on the cleansed history. Review model fit and flag product families where accuracy falls below the agreed threshold. Select the best-performing algorithm per family.

title: Commercial Overlay
description: The commercial team reviews the statistical baseline and applies adjustments based on market intelligence the model cannot see — promotions, pipeline changes, new distribution wins, or lost accounts.

title: Consensus Forecast Review
description: Commercial and supply chain align on a single consensus number. Disagreements are surfaced and resolved in the meeting — not after it. The output is one agreed forecast, not a range or multiple versions.

title: Forecast Lock & Distribution
description: The agreed consensus forecast is locked in the system and distributed to downstream planning processes. Any post-lock revision requires a formal change request with an owner and documented reason.

title: Capacity Assessment
description: Assess available production and procurement capacity for the planning horizon against the locked demand forecast. Identify bottlenecks and headroom before running the constrained plan.

title: Constrained Supply Run
description: Run the constrained supply plan in planning software to generate a feasible production and procurement schedule. The system applies prioritisation rules to allocate constrained capacity to demand.

title: Gap Identification
description: Identify where the constrained supply plan cannot meet the consensus demand plan. Gaps are quantified by volume, value, and timing and classified by root cause to guide option development.

title: Option Development
description: For each significant gap, develop costed options that could close it. The supply review should never present a gap without a proposed solution — even if none of the options are ideal.

title: Supply Plan Submission
description: Finalise and submit the supply plan for the pre-S&OP review, accompanied by the gap report and options list so the pre-S&OP team can make informed decisions.

title: Coverage Analysis
description: Assess current inventory positions against projected demand. Identify where coverage is below target, where excess stock is building, and where write-off risk exists.

title: Safety Stock Review
description: Review and update safety stock targets to reflect current demand variability, supplier lead time variability, and agreed service level targets. Targets should be challenged periodically — not left static.

title: Inventory Target Setting
description: Set or confirm cycle stock and safety stock targets for the planning horizon. Targets balance working capital efficiency against service level obligations.

title: Exception Resolution
description: Resolve inventory exceptions flagged during coverage and target-setting — shortage risks requiring expediting, excess positions requiring action, and write-off candidates requiring sign-off.

title: Resource Demand Projection
description: Translate the supply plan into resource requirements — labour, equipment, and warehouse capacity — across the planning horizon. This projection forms the basis for capacity mapping and constraint identification.

title: Capacity Mapping
description: Compare projected resource demand against available capacity to identify surplus and deficit positions. Available capacity accounts for planned maintenance, holidays, and contractual limits.

title: Constraint Identification
description: Identify which resource constraints are binding — those that will prevent the supply plan from being executed as planned. Not all constraints require action; only those that create plan risk.

title: Resource Allocation
description: Allocate available resource capacity to the supply plan, applying agreed prioritisation rules. Where constraints cannot be resolved, document the trade-off and escalate to pre-S&OP.

title: Pre-S&OP Alignment
description: The pre-S&OP meeting brings together the outputs of demand, supply, inventory, and resource planning to identify cross-functional issues that cannot be resolved within individual functions. The goal is to prepare a small number of clear decisions for executive review.

title: Financial Reconciliation
description: Reconcile the volume plan with the financial plan to ensure consistency. Significant volume-to-value gaps must be explained and, where material, resolved before executive review.

title: Executive S&OP Review
description: The executive review is the decision-making forum of the S&OP cycle. It reviews the aligned plan, considers the issues and options prepared by pre-S&OP, and makes the decisions needed to produce one agreed business plan.

title: Plan Distribution & Close
description: The agreed decisions from the executive review are applied to the plan in planning software. The final plan is distributed to all functions as the authoritative business plan. The cycle is formally closed and the next cycle is initiated.

title: The Weekly S&OE Cadence
description: A structured weekly cadence gives the S&OE process rhythm and discipline — ensuring the right reviews happen in the right order before the week's plans are committed.
text: Why cadence matters in S&OE S&OE is a high frequency process. It runs every week, which means the overhead of each cycle must be low and the structure must be tight. A weekly process that takes three days to complete leaves no time to act on decisions. A process without a fixed schedule drifts — reviews get skipped, exceptions pile up, and execution teams lose the planning signal they need. A typical weekly S&OE calendar The exact timing varies by business, but a common structure looks like this: Monday — Exception review and prioritisation Planners review the exception queue generated by planning software: what has changed since last week, what alerts have fired, what orders are at risk. The team agrees on priorities for the week. Tuesday — Demand execution review The demand team reviews the near term forecast against confirmed orders. Are this week's shipments on track? Are there customer orders that exceed the available plan? Are there demand signals suggesting a change in the coming weeks? Wednesday — Supply execution review Operations reviews the production schedule and inbound supply against the near term demand picture. Where is supply short? What needs to be expedited? What can be de prioritised to free capacity? Thursday — Integrated S&OE review The demand and supply teams meet jointly to align on the week's plan: agreed priorities, confirmed supply decisions, and a list of items requiring escalation to S&OP. This is the equivalent of the pre S&OP in the monthly cycle. Friday — Plan publication The agreed short term plan is confirmed: replenishment orders released, production schedules locked for the near term, exception resolutions communicated to execution teams. The role of planning software in the cadence Planning software makes the weekly cadence feasible by automating the data preparation. Exception queues are pre calculated. The constrained supply plan is re run automatically. Planners arrive at each review with a current picture rather than spending

title: S&OE Roles and Participants
description: S&OE is an operational process driven by planners — with limited executive involvement. Understanding who owns what in the weekly cycle prevents bottlenecks and misaligned escalation.
text: S&OE is planner driven Unlike S&OP, which requires executive participation, S&OE is driven by operational planners. The process must be able to complete — and publish a plan — without waiting for senior management. Decisions that require executive input belong in S&OP, not S&OE. Core S&OE roles Demand planner Owns the near term demand picture. Monitors confirmed orders against the weekly plan, flags deviations, and maintains the short term forecast. In S&OE, the demand planner is less concerned with the statistical model and more focused on firm orders, customer commitments, and imminent demand changes. Supply / replenishment planner Owns the near term supply plan. Manages replenishment orders, monitors inbound deliveries, and works with operations to adjust the production schedule in response to demand or supply changes. Responsible for exception resolution on supply side alerts. S&OE coordinator / IBP manager Owns the weekly calendar, facilitates the integrated review, and maintains the escalation log. Ensures the process runs on time, actions are followed up, and items requiring S&OP escalation are packaged correctly. Operations lead Represents manufacturing and logistics in the weekly supply review. Provides visibility of production constraints, quality holds, and capacity availability for the near term horizon. Who does NOT participate in S&OE S&OE is not an executive process. The CEO, CSO, and CFO do not attend weekly S&OE reviews. Their involvement in operational decisions is a symptom of either: A lack of trust in the S&OE process to resolve issues without escalation Genuine strategic issues that should be resolved in S&OP, not the weekly cycle If executives are regularly pulled into weekly operational reviews, the S&OP and S&OE process boundaries need to be re examined. Customer facing roles In businesses with key account relationships, a customer service or account management representative may participate in the demand execution review to provide visibili

title: Escalating from S&OE to S&OP
description: S&OE resolves operational deviations within the monthly plan. When a deviation is too large or persistent to manage tactically, it must be escalated to the S&OP cycle.
text: When S&OE cannot contain a problem S&OE operates within the guardrails of the S&OP plan. Most deviations — a missed delivery, a demand spike in a single week — can be absorbed through the tactical tools available to planners: expediting, reallocation, schedule adjustment. But some deviations are too large, too persistent, or too strategically significant for the weekly process to handle. Escalation to S&OP is required when: The deviation is structural — a change in demand or supply that will persist for multiple months, not just one week The financial impact is material — a service level miss that will affect a major customer or a revenue target Operational tools are exhausted — the planner has no remaining levers to close the gap within their authority A decision is needed above planner level — a trade off between service level, cost, and inventory that requires sign off from commercial or financial leadership The escalation package An escalation from S&OE to S&OP should be a structured package, not a phone call or an email. The integrated S&OE review produces a log of escalation items for each week. Each item should include: What is the problem — the gap, its size, and its projected impact What has already been tried — actions taken in S&OE and why they were insufficient What decision is needed — the specific trade off or authority required from S&OP Urgency — whether this can wait until the next monthly cycle or requires an extraordinary review Extraordinary S&OP reviews Not every escalation can wait for the monthly cycle. Planning organisations should have a defined process for calling an extraordinary S&OP review — a focused session with the necessary stakeholders to make a time sensitive decision. This should be used sparingly; if it is happening every week, the S&OP cycle itself needs adjustment. Closing the loop When S&OP makes a decision in response to an S&OE escalation, that decision must be communicated back to the S&OE team with enough clarity to update

title: Monitoring the Near-Term Plan
description: S&OE starts with visibility — knowing where the current week's plan stands before deciding where to intervene.
text: The starting point: what does the plan say? Before planners can manage exceptions, they need a clear picture of the current plan and how actuals are tracking against it. In planning software, this means opening the S&OE dashboard at the start of each week and reviewing: Confirmed orders vs. plan — are this week's orders aligned with the demand plan, above it, or below it? Inventory positions — where does stock stand relative to safety stock targets and projected coverage? Inbound supply — what deliveries are expected this week, and are any flagged as delayed? Production schedule — is manufacturing on track to meet this week's output targets? The difference between monitoring and reviewing everything Monitoring is not the same as reviewing every SKU every week. Good S&OE monitoring is exception driven: planners look at dashboards that highlight deviations from plan, not tables of every item in the supply chain. The system does the pattern recognition; the planner provides the judgment on what to do about it. Key metrics to track in S&OE Order fill rate — are customer orders being fulfilled at the committed service level? On time in full (OTIF) — are deliveries arriving when and in the quantity planned? Forecast accuracy at week level — is the near term demand signal reliable? Days of stock on hand — is inventory within the target range? Open exception count — how many unresolved exceptions are in the queue, and is the number growing or shrinking? The role of planning software dashboards A well configured S&OE dashboard in planning software surfaces the exceptions and metrics that matter for the current week's cycle. Planners should not have to build reports to get this picture — it should be available immediately when they open the system. If planners are spending the first part of every week assembling data, that is a signal that the system configuration or data infrastructure needs attention.

title: Making Adjustments and Resetting the Plan
description: When exceptions cannot be auto-resolved, planners intervene — adjusting orders, schedules, and allocations to keep the near-term plan feasible.
text: Intervention tools in S&OE When monitoring reveals a deviation from plan that requires action, the planner has several levers to work with. The choice of lever depends on the nature of the exception, the urgency, and the constraints in play. Order adjustment The most direct lever: adjust the quantity or timing of a replenishment or production order. In planning software, the planner can accept, override, or cancel system generated order recommendations. Every manual override should be documented — it forms part of the audit trail and helps calibrate the system's recommendations over time. Expediting When a supply delay threatens to cause a stock out, the planner works with procurement to bring forward an inbound delivery. Expediting has a cost — freight premium, supplier relationship strain — and should be used selectively. Planning software helps by calculating whether the expedite is actually necessary or whether available safety stock can absorb the delay. Reallocation When stock is available in one location but not another, reallocation moves inventory to where demand is highest. This requires visibility of inventory across the network — a capability that planning software provides. Reallocation decisions should be governed by allocation policy (set in S&OP) to avoid the situation where one region consistently takes stock from another without authorisation. Production schedule adjustment If demand is running above plan and capacity is available, the planner can request a production schedule change to bring forward output. This requires coordination with the operations team and must respect any minimum run sizes, changeover constraints, or material availability limitations. Demand shaping When supply is constrained and demand exceeds available inventory, the planner may work with the commercial team to shape demand: offering substitutes, prioritising higher margin or strategically important customers, or communicating lead time adjustments. This is a cross functi

title: The Integrated S&OE Review
description: The weekly integrated review aligns demand and supply on the near-term plan — resolving cross-functional conflicts and finalising the escalation list for S&OP.
text: The weekly alignment point The integrated S&OE review is the weekly equivalent of the pre S&OP in the monthly cycle. Demand and supply planners, supported by the S&OE coordinator, meet to align on the near term plan: what are the open exceptions, what has been resolved, what requires a cross functional decision, and what needs to be escalated to S&OP. This meeting typically runs 60–90 minutes. It is not a status update. It is a decision forum for the items that individual planners could not resolve on their own. Agenda structure 1. Exception queue review (15 min) How many exceptions are open? What is the priority breakdown? Which ones have been resolved since the last review? 2. Supply constrained items (20–30 min) Items where supply cannot meet near term demand. For each: what is the root cause, what options are available, what is the recommendation, and who needs to decide? 3. Demand deviations (15–20 min) Items where actual orders are running significantly above or below the near term plan. Are these one off deviations or signals of a structural change? 4. Escalation list (10–15 min) Items that cannot be resolved within the S&OE process. Each item should be documented with context and a clear articulation of what decision is needed from S&OP. 5. Plan confirmation (5 min) Confirm that the near term plan — production schedule, replenishment orders, allocation decisions — is agreed and ready to publish to execution. Who facilitates The S&OE coordinator or IBP manager facilitates the review. Their role is to maintain the agenda, prevent the discussion from diving into operational detail that belongs at the planner level, and ensure each item ends with a documented decision or action. Common failure modes Too long — a 3 hour weekly integrated review is a sign that decisions are being made that should have been resolved at the individual planner level, or that the exception queue is too large and needs better management. Missing participation — if the supply review goe

title: Handoff to Execution
description: The S&OE cycle ends when the confirmed near-term plan is published to execution teams — making the transition from planning to action clean and unambiguous.
text: The boundary between S&OE and execution S&OE manages the near term plan — typically weeks 1–6. Execution manages what happens within the current operating day or shift. The handoff between S&OE and execution is the point at which the plan transitions from something that can still be adjusted to something that is being acted on. A clean handoff means execution teams receive: A confirmed production schedule for the near term period Released replenishment and purchase orders Allocation decisions for constrained inventory Any special instructions arising from exceptions resolved in S&OE Plan publication in planning software Planning software formalises the handoff through plan publication: a step in the system that locks the near term plan and releases orders to ERP or execution systems. Once published, changes require a deliberate override — preventing ad hoc plan revisions by individuals outside the S&OE process. The publication step also triggers downstream actions: warehouse management systems receive pick and put away tasks, transportation management systems receive shipment confirmations, and manufacturing execution systems receive the production schedule. What execution teams need from S&OE Execution teams need clarity, not ambiguity. A handoff that works well gives execution: A feasible plan — not one that requires constant re planning within the day Clear priorities — when constraints exist, which orders or customers take precedence Known exceptions — any supply risks, quality holds, or availability constraints that execution needs to be aware of A contact point — who to call if something unexpected happens that requires a plan change within the day When execution finds a problem Execution teams will sometimes encounter issues that were not visible in S&OE: a machinery breakdown, a transport failure, a customer rejection. These need to feed back into S&OE quickly. A feedback channel — whether a direct call to the planner on duty, a system generated exception, o

title: What to Monitor
description: The demand signals an S&OE planner watches each week and why each one matters.
text: The S&OE planner's demand picture Demand monitoring in S&OE is not about forecasting the future — that belongs to S&OP. It is about tracking what is actually happening against the plan that was agreed last month, and catching deviations early enough to do something about them. The S&OE planner does this by watching a set of near term demand signals every week. Actual orders vs. plan by SKU and location The primary signal is the comparison of confirmed customer orders against the demand plan at the SKU and location level. At the aggregate family level, noise often cancels out. At the SKU level, it does not. A product that is running 30% above plan for three consecutive weeks is not noise — it is a signal that the plan is wrong and supply needs to respond. The S&OE planner reviews this comparison weekly, not to revise the forecast (that happens in S&OP), but to assess whether current supply commitments can still be met, and whether exceptions need to be raised now. Near term forecast deviation Most planning systems maintain a rolling short horizon forecast — typically a 4 to 8 week view updated from actual order intake. Where the near term forecast has shifted materially since the last S&OE cycle, the planner flags this as a deviation. A positive deviation (demand higher than planned) creates a supply risk. A negative deviation (demand lower than planned) may signal inventory build up or the need to defer production. Demand spikes and dips Sudden one week spikes and dips are tracked separately from trend deviations. A spike may represent a large customer order, a promotion pulling forward demand, or a data entry error. A dip may indicate a customer delivery delay, order cancellation, or an inventory correction at the customer's end. The planner's job is to determine the cause before taking action — responding to a data error as if it were a real demand shift causes unnecessary disruption. Customer order confirmation rate For make to order environments, the share of ex

title: Monitoring in Practice
description: How demand monitoring works inside planning software — workspaces, exception queues, and the actions available per exception type.
text: The S&OE demand monitoring workspace Planning software surfaces demand monitoring through a dedicated workspace or view, typically labelled along the lines of "S&OE Demand" or "Near Term Demand Monitor." This workspace is distinct from the S&OP demand planning module — it is scoped to the near term horizon (usually the next four to eight weeks) and is oriented around exceptions rather than plan creation. The workspace typically shows a grid of SKUs and locations with colour coded deviation indicators, alongside an exception queue that lists items that have breached the configured alert thresholds. The planner starts each monitoring cycle here, working through the exception queue before scanning the wider deviation grid. Exception queues filtered to demand type exceptions Planning systems classify exceptions by type. Demand type exceptions include: demand deviation above threshold, demand deviation below threshold, order confirmation rate below threshold, and near term forecast revision above threshold. The exception queue can be filtered to show only demand exceptions, separating them from supply and inventory exceptions that are handled by different parts of the S&OE process. Each exception record shows the affected item, the nature of the deviation, the current value versus the plan value, and the severity flag — typically a traffic light rating based on how far the deviation exceeds the configured tolerance. High severity exceptions appear at the top of the queue and require same day attention. Reading demand deviation charts For each exception item, the planner can open a deviation chart that shows the full short horizon demand picture: the baseline plan (the agreed S&OP number), the current near term forecast, and the actual order intake to date. The chart makes it visually clear whether the deviation is a one week spike or a sustained trend, and whether it is worsening or stabilising. The shape of the deviation matters. A single spike that has already passed w

title: Escalation from Demand Monitoring
description: When demand deviations exceed what S&OE can absorb — the thresholds, decision logic, and communication steps for escalating to S&OP.
text: Two escalation thresholds Demand monitoring in S&OE operates with two distinct escalation thresholds, not one. The first threshold triggers escalation within S&OE — the deviation is too large to absorb silently, so it goes to the integrated S&OE review for a cross functional decision. The second threshold triggers escalation beyond S&OE into S&OP — the deviation is large enough, persistent enough, or structurally significant enough that a monthly plan revision is needed. Conflating these two thresholds is a common error. Not every demand deviation that warrants attention in S&OE needs to go to S&OP. The S&OE planner's job is to resolve what can be resolved within the near term plan and to escalate only what cannot. The decision logic The central question the S&OE planner answers when evaluating a demand exception is: can this deviation be absorbed within the current plan? Absorption is possible when available stock, near term production capacity, or scheduled inbound supply can cover the demand gap without compromising other customer commitments. If the deviation is upward (demand higher than plan) and available to promise inventory is sufficient, the plan can accommodate the additional demand without a formal revision. Absorption is not possible when: Demand is materially above plan and available supply is already committed to other orders. Demand is materially below plan and production already running cannot be economically deferred or redirected. The deviation has persisted for multiple consecutive weeks, indicating the plan itself is structurally wrong. The deviation affects a high priority customer, a constrained material, or a product with long replenishment lead times. When absorption is not possible, the planner escalates. The choice of escalation path — S&OE review or S&OP — depends on whether the response requires near term operational adjustment alone, or a revision to the baseline plan that only the S&OP cycle can authorise. S&OE review escalation When a

title: Supply Signals
description: The supply signals an S&OE planner tracks each week and how each feeds the monitoring picture.
text: What supply monitoring covers Supply monitoring in S&OE is the counterpart to demand monitoring. Where demand monitoring asks "is what customers want aligned with what the plan said they would want?", supply monitoring asks "is what the supply chain is delivering aligned with what the plan said it would deliver?" Both questions must be answered weekly for the integrated S&OE picture to be complete. Supply monitoring tracks four categories of signal: inbound purchase orders, production output, inventory position, and quality holds. Inbound purchase orders For each active purchase order in the near term horizon, the S&OE planner monitors two metrics: on time delivery rate and quantity confirmation. On time delivery rate measures whether suppliers are confirming delivery on the committed date. A supplier who has delivered on time for twelve consecutive weeks is a low risk item in the monitoring picture. A supplier who has missed three of the last five committed dates is a risk that requires active tracking, regardless of whether there is currently a confirmed delay for the next delivery. Quantity confirmation tracks whether the supplier has confirmed they will deliver the full ordered quantity. Partial confirmations — suppliers signalling they will deliver less than ordered — are a leading indicator of potential stock outs and need to be resolved well before the delivery date arrives. Production output vs. schedule Where supply involves internal manufacturing or co manufacturing, the planner tracks actual production output against the production schedule. Underperformance against schedule depletes available inventory faster than the plan assumed. Overperformance, while often welcome, can create inventory build up if demand is running below plan. The relevant metrics are schedule adherence (percentage of scheduled production completed on time) and yield rate (units produced as a proportion of units started, where quality losses reduce usable output). Both affect the sup

title: Supply Exceptions and Response
description: How supply exceptions surface in planning software, what responses are available, and when the planner acts versus escalates.
text: How supply exceptions surface Planning software surfaces supply exceptions through the same exception queue mechanism used for demand exceptions, but filtered to supply type alerts. Common supply exception types include: late inbound PO, quantity shortfall on confirmed PO, inventory below safety stock, projected stock out within horizon, and schedule adherence below threshold. Each exception appears with the affected item, the nature of the problem, the severity rating, and the relevant planning data — current stock position, open supply orders, and projected inventory trajectory. The planner works through the supply exception queue as part of the weekly S&OE cycle, typically after the demand monitoring review, so that the demand picture is already established when assessing supply responses. Response options available in planning software Planning software gives the S&OE planner several direct response actions for supply exceptions. The right action depends on the nature of the exception and the alternatives available in the current supply picture. Expediting an inbound PO involves contacting the supplier (or routing the request through procurement) to pull forward a confirmed delivery date. In the planning system, the planner can update the expected receipt date to reflect the expedited commitment, which immediately updates the projected inventory position. Expediting has a cost — it may incur premium freight charges or disrupt the supplier's own schedule — and should be reserved for situations where the inventory risk justifies that cost. Reallocating available stock is the appropriate response when one location or customer segment has a surplus and another has a shortfall. The planner identifies available stock in the system, initiates a stock transfer order, and updates the allocation to redirect supply toward the at risk demand.
